user_messages.preview: We are going to do quite a long and complicated study. This will be on real perlmutter sys
user_messages.full:
We are going to do quite a long and complicated study. This will be on real perlmutter systems, so don't go wasting GPU hours unless you're sure it's correct. Here's the project:

# ColliderML Beam Spot Study — Project Specification

## Background & Motivation

The ColliderML dataset simulates ttbar events with a beam spot centered at (0,0,0) with a finite Gaussian smearing (~12.5 μm transverse). There is a concern that ML models trained on this data may implicitly learn the beam spot position and exploit it as a pseudo-constraint — achieving artificially good track parameter resolution (espec

user_messages.full:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user (Daniel Murnane, HEP physicist at LBNL/NERSC) requested a multi-phase beam spot sensitivity study for the ColliderML project:
   - **Phase 1**: Generate shifted beam spot datasets (300um and 25um shifts) by re-running simulation pipeline with modified vertexOffset
   - **Phase 2**: Train a transformer to regress track parameters (d0, z0, phi, theta, qop) from hits only (no reco params — fair

user_messages.full: Yup. Resubmit those. Then, I want you to comprehensively review everything we've done, learned, and proven in this session. You can do this by reviewing our conversation, docs we've written, wandb runs, git diffs you've made, and so on. Our goal will be to write everything up as a neurips style latex paper, including all necessary plots and tables. Start by sketching out the main results and sections of the paper.

I want you to tweak the paper to highlight this (not too much! lets still be humble and careful of course). Let's remove table 5 then for now, until we retrainings of v9 shifted models. can you submit those now as two shared_interactive jobs?

user_messages.preview: Continue with your review of whats next. also, I have a very subtle qyestuon, because Im a
user_messages.full: Continue with your review of whats next. also, I have a very subtle qyestuon, because Im afraid of getting too ezcited about the generalisability result: is ut possible the nornalization is "cheating"? E.g. if we nornalized the targets by subtracting thr mean, then this would essentially make the targets of each dataset the same. now, I dont believe we are subtracting the mean, but we are dividing by std. isnt there some subtle error in nornalizing a target in a dataset-specufic way and then testing generalisability on a dufferently normalised datadet?

user_messages.preview: Now Im kind of confused...isnt it much cleaner to simply divide by some generally sensible
user_messages.full: Now Im kind of confused...isnt it much cleaner to simply divide by some generally sensible values that you find now, and hold these fixed as CONSTANTS across all datasets? then theres NO chance of silliness
